item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be frank, I do not care about thing out there any longer.

Just wondering if this is intended?

Also, I noticed he uses different signatures, but I can't make out why, even though I'm pretty sure that's intentional and I'm supposed to figure something out here.

I also would have appreciated a more detailed description of the thing, maybe regarding color, texture, etc.

As one earlier comment pointed out, this has almost too much going on. But I still liked it and felt entertained and intrigued while reading it, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mann's journal really hooked me up, very good! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: can we not shit on autistic people please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow. this is really interesting and the twist here is wild, i love it. i kind of get the impression there is no beast at all and it's the region itself that's anomalous, but that'd mean the Foundation would probably describe it differently if they found the journals, so i'm not sure how that works. aside from that, good read, enjoyed it, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea itself is… adequate. But as others have said: the tone is way off from the introduction of the log onwards. This Mann guy thinks and acts like a robot, he seems like a barely functional autistic person and it's a bore to read him. No emotion, no personality, and whatever "humanity" is there in the logs is corny and hamfisted as hell (Me terrified, horrifying tentacles, terror, horror, scary horror). The logs are longwinded and tedious, the payoff is predictable and minimal. I wouldn't be lying if I told you that the skip would work better if you just erased all the logs and left it like that, really. I'll have to downvote.

item.title: "No, Joe, you ARE the tentacles!"
item.content:encoded:
This one is very, very interesting. I think there's a lot to say about it… but (fittingly) all of the words elude me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Toadking, thanks for reading. I want to respond to your comments here.

The logs are overly long

I have to say I disagree. When I wrote this, I purposefully wrote longer logs and intentionally put unnecessary information in them to help the reader gain attachment to Mann and understand his character better, as well as see how his character was developing.

I get not wanting to freak his people out …

As you stated, I may not have been clear as to when this log was supposed to have occurred. But my intention as the writer was for the reader to believe that this happened somewhere between 1900-1920. As such, the Foundation was a much younger organisation with a lot less experience in the field of anomalies, and so by extension its leaders were also subject to making faulty decisions especially in the field, and under the effects of an anomaly no less.

I'd recommend streamlining them down to a tighter few entries

I will re-read them with your critique in mind and determine whether I want to do this.

Thank you again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: double post due to page error, staff please delete

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it but I have notes

+ notes
- notes

The area should then there on out be monitored for

is an oddly worded sentence. "there on out" seems unnecessary imo.

simple utility actions such as picking objects up.

Similarly, "such as picking objects up" is odd wording and unnecessary.

The logs are overly long, with 3 days each, often 3+ entries per day, with nothing especially happening to keep things moving. A lot of, "made camp, getting closer" updates. His wording strikes me as out of place a bit, a little too much like a Lovecraftian. There's no date hinted at here, so I had assumed this was a more modern event.

I also have some gripes about him not mentioning the voices to his companions right off the bat. I get not wanting to freak his people out, BUT any trained Foundation agent shouldn't be concerned with that. First order of business would be, "Hey, I'm hearing stuff, anyone else? Yeah? Probably that creature in the distance. Got it."

All in all I loved the first half, but the logs don't do it for me. I'd recommend streamlining them down to a tighter few entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that's some good Lovecraftian Antartic Time Displacement Body Horror! Nicely done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a containment-is-a-pain-in-the-rear type keter: it periodically moves around, and they have to figure out where it went, and go patrol a different part of the middle of nowhere in Antarctica for a while until it goes back to its usual place. Which is also in the middle of nowhere in Antarctica.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 but Is it keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hadn't read this before. It reminds me of the pulp short story, "In Amundsen's Tent".

In a good way, I hasten to add.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was certainly my take on it, particularly given he had a tissue sample lopped off of him under circumstances similar to when he did it to the beastie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgive my slowness, but what happened here? Did Joseph Mann turn into the tentacle thing somehow? or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback, I've been wondering how to fix that little issue. Going to implement that now, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea, the way the logs build to the reveal. But that repeated use of the word "flicker" put me off at first. It's too anecdotal rather than technical in tone, especially as I get my first impression of the article. "Spontaneous spacial relocation" or the like might work better. (Perhaps someone can suggest something that's less of a mouthful.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has some good ideas but is hampered by tonal problems ("took it upon himself") and an interminable, repetitious and narmy log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Four different collapses are a bit much, especially when many, many days are "we walked, nothing happened" and "I heard the voice and nothing happened".
It also has a lot going on (as in, it is a huge entity that teleports and also double shifts in size, that reshapes space and time, that also projects voices in english but only if you are close enough, that also makes people turn slightly insane). If there is a really unifying factor here, a single effect that just causes all of these symptoms beyond "Well, space/time distortion" handwaving, I am not seeing it.

Additional thought: What does this really do for the MTF? It is a report by a MIA commander who doesn't really achieve anything besides walking and who doesn't even call his team mates by name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tonal issues, and "flicker" is never really explained. The idea of it being immeasurable outside a certain radius is neat, but not enough to carry the whole article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I went through the logs, I remembered a story I'd once read - by Lovecraft, I think.

A guy goes into the woods, finds a little girl, shoots her attacker, takes her home, realizes she is a monster of some kind, and drags her out into the woods to kill her, only to be shot by his past self, and the loop continued.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna come through and do a line-by-line this weekend if no one else beats me to it, but let me just say that I want to like it, but it has too many things going against it for me to upvote yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely drew heavy inspiration from Lovecraft on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree the tone is a little strange but I'm pretty sure it's supposed to be aping the pulpy old timey lovecraft type of narration so I can excuse it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idea is good, but tone is way off. That ruins it for me, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done, this. I think you did a fine job of tying those last journal entries up with what had come prior. Antarctic horror is right up my alley, and you did the genre proud.

edit: I don't know if that guy really needed to sign his name so often for what is obviously his travel log for a singular mission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The gradual breakdown in the journals is what really drew me in here.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first i thought it was a giant entity like the one from the SCP-004-12 test.
But this is completely different and utterly creepy.
I like it a lot!
